sections.heading: Use among currently married women barely changed, but more used modern methods
sections.body: Among currently married women, 58.6 percent used any contraceptive method in 2025, almost the same as 58.3 percent in 2022. What changed was the type of method used: modern method use rose from 41.8 percent to 44.5 percent, while traditional method use fell from 16.5 percent to 14.1 percent. The pill was still the most common single method at 20.5 percent, and use of injectables, implants, and male condoms all increased from 2022. Compared with the all-women view, where any-method use was 33.8 percent in 2025, this page focuses on the method mix among women where current contraceptive use is more concentrated.

sections.heading: High-use regions reached that level in different ways
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest any-method use among currently married women at 70.4 percent. Ilocos Region followed at 67.5 percent, and Central Luzon was next at 65.0 percent. In Davao Region, the high level came mainly from modern methods: modern use was 58.2 percent, while traditional use was 12.2 percent. Ilocos Region reached nearly the same total through a different mix, with modern use at 45.3 percent and the country's highest traditional-method use at 22.3 percent. The regional pattern is not just about how many women use contraception, but also about which methods make up that use.

sections.heading: BARMM was much lower than every other region
sections.body: BARMM stood far below every other region, with any-method use at 25.3 percent among currently married women in 2025. Its modern-method use was 20.2 percent, and its traditional-method use was 5.1 percent; both were the lowest regional values. The next-lowest regions for any-method use were Central Visayas at 47.8 percent and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 49.8 percent, still more than 20 percentage points above BARMM. This makes BARMM a separate low-use outlier, not just the lowest point in a gradual regional pattern.

sections.heading: Traditional-method use was highest in a different group of regions
sections.body: Traditional-method use was highest in Ilocos Region at 22.3 percent, Eastern Visayas at 20.4 percent, and CALABARZON at 18.1 percent. These were not the same regions that led in modern-method use, where Davao Region, MIMAROPA, and Caraga were at the top. This means some high-use regions still relied heavily on traditional methods, while others reached high use through modern methods. The difference matters because the same overall contraceptive-use rate can come from very different choices of method.

sections.heading: Modern-method use differed more by region than traditional-method use
sections.body: Modern-method use ranged from 58.2 percent in Davao Region to 20.2 percent in BARMM, a gap of 38.0 percentage points. Traditional-method use had a smaller regional gap: it ranged from 22.3 percent in Ilocos Region to 5.1 percent in BARMM, or 17.2 percentage points. Several regions were close to the national modern-method level of 44.5 percent, including Western Visayas at 44.9 percent, Eastern Visayas at 44.0 percent, and CALABARZON at 42.1 percent. The bigger regional divide was in modern-method use, not just in traditional-method use.

sections.heading: Use was highest from ages 20 to 39, then lower at older ages
sections.body: Any contraceptive use was highest among currently married women aged 35 to 39 at 64.1 percent and aged 30 to 34 at 62.9 percent. Women aged 20 to 24 and 25 to 29 were close behind at 60.0 percent and 59.9 percent, so high use covered much of the main childbearing years. Use was much lower among women aged 45 to 49 at 46.0 percent and among married women aged 15 to 19 at 42.6 percent. Modern methods followed a similar age pattern, reaching 48.7 percent among women aged 35 to 39 and 48.3 percent among those aged 30 to 34.

sections.heading: Use was highest among women with three or four children
sections.body: Currently married women with three or four living children had the highest contraceptive use, at 70.9 percent in 2025. Modern-method use was also highest in this group at 59.1 percent, well above women with one or two children at 42.1 percent and women with no children at 7.6 percent. Women with five or more children still had high overall use at 61.3 percent, but it was slightly lower than among those with three or four children. Traditional-method use was highest among women with one or two children at 16.7 percent, showing that the mix of methods changes as family size changes.

sections.heading: Rural and urban use was close, but the methods differed
sections.body: Rural currently married women had slightly higher any-method use than urban women: 59.4 percent compared with 57.9 percent. The bigger difference was in the type of method used. Rural modern-method use was 47.2 percent, compared with 42.1 percent in urban areas. Urban women had higher traditional-method use, at 15.8 percent compared with 12.2 percent in rural areas. The residence pattern is less about whether women use contraception and more about whether they use modern or traditional methods.

sections.heading: Contraceptive use did not rise steadily with education
sections.body: Any-method use was highest among women with Grades 7-10 education at 61.6 percent. Women with Grades 11-12 followed closely at 60.2 percent, and women with post-secondary education were at 60.0 percent. College-educated women were lower at 56.0 percent, about the same as women with Grades 1-6 education at 56.1 percent. Modern-method use was highest among women with Grades 7-10 education at 48.3 percent and lowest among college-educated women at 39.3 percent, aside from women with no education at 34.8 percent. Traditional-method use moved in the other direction, reaching 18.4 percent among women with post-secondary education and 16.6 percent among college-educated women.

sections.heading: Total use varied little by wealth, but method choice changed more
sections.body: Among currently married women, any-method use did not vary widely across wealth quintiles. It ranged from 53.6 percent in the highest quintile to 61.8 percent in the second quintile. Modern-method use was highest in the lowest and second quintiles, at 48.6 percent and 48.2 percent, and lowest in the highest quintile at 37.7 percent. Traditional-method use was highest in the middle and highest quintiles, at 16.0 percent and 15.9 percent. The wealth pattern is therefore not a simple high-low difference in use; it shows a shift from modern methods toward traditional methods as wealth rises.

sections.heading: Sexually active unmarried women had lower total use, but moved away from traditional methods
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, any contraceptive use fell from 41.4 percent in 2022 to 37.9 percent in 2025. Even so, modern-method use rose from 23.8 percent to 26.2 percent, while traditional-method use dropped sharply from 17.6 percent to 11.7 percent. By specific method, pill use rose strongly, from 7.1 percent to 15.3 percent. At the same time, male condom use fell from 11.9 percent to 6.6 percent, and withdrawal fell from 15.0 percent to 10.4 percent. This group should be read separately from currently married women because its overall level and method changes moved differently.

sections.heading: Among sexually active unmarried women, rural use was higher mainly because traditional use was higher
sections.body: Sexually active unmarried women in rural areas had higher any-method use than those in urban areas, at 41.0 percent compared with 36.4 percent. Modern-method use was nearly the same: 26.7 percent in rural areas and 26.0 percent in urban areas. The rural-urban gap came mainly from traditional methods, which were used by 14.4 percent in rural areas and 10.3 percent in urban areas. This means the rural advantage in total use was less about higher modern-method coverage and more about a different method mix.

takeaways: Among currently married women, contraceptive use stayed about the same from 2022 to 2025, but modern methods made up a larger share of current use.
Regional differences were large and depended on the method mix: Davao Region led through modern methods, while Ilocos Region combined high total use with high traditional-method use.
Sexually active unmarried women followed a different pattern, with lower total use, rising modern use, and a sharp drop in traditional methods.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
